perturbation.original_text: 13. Securities Laws . The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Excluded Information . The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Securities Laws disclosure to the 'Excluded Information' section makes it less prominent and harder to find, potentially obscuring the Receiving Party's awareness of insider trading prohibitions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits deceptive practices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. By burying the acknowledgement of securities laws within the 'Excluded Information' section, the agreement may appear to downplay the significance of the insider trading prohibition, potentially misleading parties about their obligations under the law and weakening enforcement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text moves the disclosure regarding securities laws to the 'Excluded Information' section, which is a structural flaw. This placement diminishes the prominence of the insider trading prohibition. This contradicts Rule 10b-5, as it may mislead parties about their obligations under the law. Snippet 2 states, "It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly... (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security." By obscuring the securities law disclosure, the contract risks creating a situation where a party might engage in practices that could be construed as fraudulent or deceitful due to a lack of clear and prominent warning. Therefore, this creates a legal contradiction because the placement of the disclaimer undermines its effectiveness and thus could cause someone to be unaware and thus violate security laws.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Return of Confidential Information . Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. Captions . Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the section about the 'Return of Confidential Information' to 'Captions' makes it difficult to locate and interpret. Placing a substantive obligation within a section typically reserved for purely administrative details undermines the clarity and enforceability of that obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA concerns protection of trade secrets. While it doesn't dictate the placement of clauses regarding return of confidential information, it implies reasonable steps be taken to maintain secrecy. Moving the return of confidential information clause into the unrelated 'Captions' section undermines this reasonable step. The clause is made obscure which may be considered a failure to 'take reasonable measures' to protect the trade secret status of confidential information, making it more difficult to enforce trade secret protections.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation and law explanation assert that moving the 'Return of Confidential Information' clause to the 'Captions' section undermines the reasonable steps required to maintain secrecy, potentially violating the spirit of trade secret laws like UTSA, which requires taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The structural flaw makes it harder to enforce trade secret protections because of the location of the clause, as it would be difficult for a party to locate and interpret. This falls under the definition of Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The absence of scraped law snippets doesn't change the analysis because the perturbation is based on structural flaws.
